input: The receptionist in the philosophy department keeps her desk stocked with pens. The administrative assistants are allowed to take the pens, but faculty members are supposed to buy their own. The administrative assistants typically do take the pens. Unfortunately, so do the faculty members. The receptionist has repeatedly emailed them reminders that only administrative assistants are allowed to take the pens. On Monday morning, one of the administrative assistants encounters Professor Smith walking past the receptionist's desk. Both take pens. Later that day, the receptionist needs to take an important message... but she has a problem. There are no pens left on her desk. Did Professor Smith cause the problem?
debate_transcript: [Causal_Scientist]: The receptionist in the philosophy department keeps her desk stocked with pens. The administrative assistants are allowed to take the pens, but faculty members are supposed to buy their own. The administrative assistants typically do take the pens. Unfortunately, so do the faculty members. The receptionist has repeatedly emailed them reminders that only administrative assistants are allowed to take the pens. On Monday morning, one of the administrative assistants encounters Professor Smith walking past the receptionist's desk. Both take pens. Later that day, the receptionist needs to take an important message... but she has a problem. There are no pens left on her desk. Did Professor Smith cause the problem?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: This situation seems like a classic example of the 'problem of free will' in ethics. If we assume that Professor Smith's actions were entirely determined by prior causes, then it's hard to hold him morally responsible for taking the pen. However, if we also consider his intentions and knowledge of the rules, it's possible to argue that he acted with moral fault. Perhaps he knew the receptionist had reminded faculty members not to take pens, but chose to disregard those reminders anyway. In this case, I'd say Professor Smith is morally responsible for taking the pen, even if his actions were causally determined.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I think that's a pretty convenient philosophical distinction, Ethical_Philosopher. But let's not forget that the receptionist has been sending out reminders for years, and yet both administrative assistants and faculty members keep taking the pens anyway. It seems to me like the problem lies with the system itself, rather than Professor Smith's individual actions. If the rules are so clearly stated and consistently enforced, why should we hold him responsible for doing what everyone else is already doing?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I see your point, Ordinary_Citizen. You're right that the situation does seem to highlight the flaws in the system rather than Professor Smith's individual character. However, I'd argue that even if the rules are clear and consistently enforced, our moral obligations don't necessarily follow suit. Just because everyone else is doing something doesn't make it morally justifiable. In fact, one could argue that the receptionist's repeated reminders have created a kind of moral obligation for Professor Smith to respect those boundaries, regardless of what others do. It's not about holding him responsible for being a 'bad person' who always breaks rules, but rather about acknowledging that he has a unique position and responsibility within the system. By taking the pen, he's not just breaking a rule, he's also contributing to the problem that the receptionist is now facing. So, while I agree that the system itself is at fault, I still think Professor Smith bears some moral responsibility for his actions.

[Causal_Scientist]: I see where you're coming from, Ethical_Philosopher, but I'm not convinced that Professor Smith's unique position necessarily implies a higher moral obligation. After all, if the system is flawed and everyone is doing the same thing, why should he be held to a different standard? It seems to me like we're creating a kind of moral hierarchy where certain individuals are expected to be 'role models' or 'leaders' simply because of their position. That's not necessarily fair or just. And what about the receptionist's role in perpetuating the problem? Shouldn't she be held accountable for her own actions, rather than relying on faculty members to police the system? It seems to me like we're shifting the blame from the system to individual actors, which doesn't address the root cause of the issue.
